nativeRange.habitat: Large rivers and lakes; occurs in Lake Tanganyika and throughout the Congo River basin, including estuarine zones

diet.notes: Requires hard-shelled foods to wear down continuously growing teeth — frozen large prawns, mussels, crab claws, and snails are strongly recommended. Softer foods (fish flesh, worms) can supplement but cannot replace hard-shell items. Dental overgrowth leads to starvation if hard foods are not regularly provided.

imageCandidates.notes: Tiia Monto, 2244x1138. Full lateral profile of a vibrant live adult showing the complete olive-yellow body with intricate dark maze/labyrinth vermiculation, pale belly, and the diagnostic bright-yellow spread caudal fin. Sharpest, most complete diagnostic view.
Frank Vincentz, 5616x3744. Dynamic three-quarter swimming shot, tack-sharp, yellow caudal fin fully extended and maze pattern crisp across the flank; different angle from the profile pick.
Amada44, 3262x2206. Frontal view showing the beak-like fused teeth and facial maze pattern; sharp and well-lit, adds a distinct head-on angle.

summary: The MBU Puffer (Tetraodon mbu) is widely considered the largest freshwater puffer species, reaching up to 75 cm. Native to Lake Tanganyika and the Congo River basin, it demands a 2,000-litre custom setup, solitary housing, and a strict hard-shell diet — a commitment suited only to the most dedicated aquarists.
careNotes:
The MBU Puffer is widely considered the largest freshwater puffer species. Reported maximum lengths range from 67 cm to 75 cm, and keepers are advised to plan for the larger end when sizing their setup. A tank of at least 2,000 litres — roughly 240 cm long — is generally regarded as the absolute floor for a single adult, and many experienced keepers recommend going larger still.

This species must be kept alone. Its beak-like fused teeth can inflict serious injuries on any tankmate, and the MBU Puffer is widely regarded as incompatible with all fish, invertebrates, and conspecifics alike. Hard-shelled prey — mussels, crab claws, whole snails — must be offered regularly to keep the teeth worn down. Dental overgrowth is one of the leading causes of starvation in captive specimens, because teeth that grow unchecked eventually prevent the fish from eating.

MBU Puffers have been widely reported as highly intelligent and capable of recognising individual keepers, often approaching the glass and displaying what hobbyists describe as interactive or 'begging' behaviour. However, they are commonly sold as appealing juveniles only a few centimetres long, and they grow quickly. The custom-built system this species ultimately requires makes impulse purchases a serious animal welfare concern.

Tetrodotoxin is present in the flesh, as it is in all pufferfish; this is a concern only for human consumption and is not released into the water column during normal keeping.

Water chemistry should accommodate both Lake Tanganyika populations (which favour harder, more alkaline water) and Congo River populations (which can tolerate softer, more neutral conditions). A pH of 6.8–8.0 with moderate hardness is broadly accepted as suitable across the range. No successful captive breeding has been documented.
breedingNotes: No successful captive breeding has been documented. The sheer volume of water required and the species' aggressive nature make controlled breeding exceptionally difficult; captive specimens are wild-caught or F1 offspring.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish
primary.notes: Primary source used after Aquarium Co-Op (designated primary for fish-freshwater slice) returned HTTP 404 for this species. SeriouslyFish provides comprehensive peer-reviewed husbandry data including tank dimensions, water parameters, diet and compatibility.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (max 67 cm vs SeriouslyFish 75 cm — spread acceptable, used 60-75 cm range), temperature (24-26°C agrees), native range (Lake Tanganyika + Congo basin confirmed), taxonomy (Order Tetraodontiformes confirmed).

fish.breedingNotes: No captive breeding documented; specimens in trade are wild-caught.
